narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid LSAT score and full-time study plans, we understand how disappointing it must be to receive this rejection from our law school admissions process. Our model, trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicted a 75% chance that you would fail it on your first try, which is the threshold we use to make admission decisions—anything above that risk level leads to rejection to ensure we select candidates likely to succeed in their legal careers. This prediction stemmed primarily from your academic performance in school, particularly your lower decile rankings in Year 1 (3rd decile, below the typical 6th) and Year 3 (2nd decile, well under the average 6th), which were the strongest factors pushing the model toward forecasting bar exam struggles. These early and later grade positions signaled to the model a pattern of underperformance compared to most applicants, who rank higher in their school's deciles. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.80, also below the average of 3.21, added further weight toward the failure prediction, reinforcing concerns about sustaining the high academic demands of law school and the bar. On a brighter note, your LSAT score of 38—stronger than the typical 37—worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success and showing your potential in standardized testing. Your lower-middle family income bracket similarly nudged things positively, as the model associates it with resilience in some cases, though not enough to offset the academics. Being a full-time student aligned with the vast majority (over 90%) who succeed, providing a small boost too. Overall, your profile stands out from typical admitted students mainly due to those weaker school deciles and GPA, which overshadowed your LSAT strength. While this stings, it's not the end of your path to law—consider retaking courses to boost your deciles, aiming for a higher undergrad-equivalent GPA if possible, or gaining targeted test prep experience to demonstrate growth. We encourage you to apply to programs with holistic reviews or reapply after strengthening these areas; many students like you turn predictions around with focused effort. You're clearly capable, and with the right steps, you can prove the model wrong. Keep pursuing your goals—we're rooting for you.
